figures.caption: Table 7: AUROC (mean ± std, 5-fold CV) on the Early Stage Diabetes Risk Dataset (N = 520, 2 silos). Bold = proposed FedTGNN-SS. FedTGNN-SS significantly outperforms all GCN-based methods at 80% missing.
Table 1: Comparison of FedTGNN-SS with representative prior works. ✓= supported; ×= not supported; ∼ = partial.
Table 9: Macro-F1 (mean ± std, 5-fold CV) on Pima Indians. Bold = proposed FedTGNN-SS.
Table 10: Ablation Study on Pima (ρ = 0.80, 5-fold CV).

Figure 1: Overview of the proposed FedTGNN-SS framework. Each hospital trains a local GNN on its patient similarity graph with (i) prototype-guided pseudo-labeling, (ii) adaptive graph refinement, and (iii) clinical-aware augmentation; only class-level prototypes are shared with the server for privacy-safe aggregation across rounds.

Table 5: AUROC (mean± std, 5-fold CV) on the GDM Dataset (N = 3,525, 3 silos). Bold = proposed FedTGNN-SS (not necessarily best due to ceiling effect). † = centralized oracle. All methods achieve > 0.98 AUROC due to the high discriminative power of OGTT glucose (ceiling effect).

Table 6: AUROC (mean ± std, 5-fold CV) on the Pima Indians Diabetes Dataset (N = 768, 2 silos). Bold = proposed FedTGNN-SS. FedTGNN-SS ranks #1 at 10%–50% missing and #2 at 80% missing among all federated privacy-preserving methods.
